body.body:
Igor named the language: Engram. From Semon (1904) — the engram is the physical trace memory leaves in neural tissue; the engram IS the memory, not a pointer to it. In Engram: you don't write code that runs separately from memory — you lay down template nodes that become the substrate. The engram IS the program. At runtime, there is only activation spreading through engrams (habits).

Characteristics to document: biologically-inspired, template macros (not subroutines), reactive trigger→condition→action, code=data (habits are Memory nodes), no call stack, graph-structured, async by default, gradients not binary control flow, self-modifying via template instantiation.

Closest ancestors: Lisp macros (code=data, macro expansion), dataflow programming, reactive systems, Hebb's rule (wiring IS the program).

Output: design_docs/engram_language.md — name etymology, grammar, full pattern inventory (20 patterns — original 10 + Igor's 10 additions), paradigm comparison, example programs.
body.title: Engram language spec — name, grammar, primitives, paradigm doc
body.result: design_docs/engram_language.md written: 429-line full spec. Covers name etymology (Semon 1904), core paradigm table, grammar (habit/template/condition/action node schemas), 12 primitive ops, all 21 patterns with descriptions and canonical instances, 3 example programs, paradigm comparison vs Lisp/Dataflow/Erlang/SQL, implementation status table, and 6 design principles. BLOOM_INHIBIT and DISTILLATION given full clarifications from Igor.
